text: The surgeon positions three ports for the right hemicolectomy. The vasilio-psychocolic tent is extended, and the duodenal window is identified and opened. Using traction and counter-traction with two forceps, the surgeon exposes the upper mesenteric vein, facilitating lymphadenectomy of the right colon sheath.

The surgeon performs an extended DII lymphadenectomy for disease staging, identifying and clamping the hypochloric vessels, arteries, and veins. The second and initial third portions of the duodenum are highlighted and released, along with the body and head of the pancreas. The fasciae of Tod and Gerota are kept intact during the dissociation, while the colic vessels on the right, including arteries and veins, are released, ligated, and sectioned.

The surgeon completes the lymphadenectomy of the right colon, highlighting and dissecting a branch of the trunk of L, which is tied and sectioned. Gauze is placed for hemostatic purposes during the dissection of the transverse mesocolon, isolating and sectioning the right branches of the medial colic artery. The marginal branches of the medial colic artery are exposed, and the right colon wall is highlighted. The omentum is sectioned and removed in block with the operating piece, followed by the detachment of the epiproic colon.

The surgeon performs a DIII lymphadenectomy, focusing on the lower margin of the gastroepiploic vessels on the right. The previously sectioned branch of the colic of the gastroepiploic is visible, and the surgeon proceeds with further dissection of the coloepiploic area, lowering and dissociating the right hepatic flexure. The peritoneal margin leading into the Morrison's pouch is maintained, and the parietocolic ligament on the right is incised.

The surgeon incises the parietocolic ligament on the right side, facilitating the section of the transverse colon using a blue load stapler. The root of the mesentery is opened, and the parietocolic dissociation continues with the complete opening of the mesocolon root. The mesoileum is mobilized and sectioned, using either a combined ultrasound device or separate ligation of the artery and vein, approximately ten centimeters from the ileocecal valve.

The surgeon completes the right hemicolectomy using a no-touch technique and proceeds to package the anastomosis. The anastomosis is performed using a lateral-lateral isoperistatic technique with a linear suture approximately 4.5 centimeters in diameter. The closure of the anastomosis is achieved with a chiseled or continuous point using monofilament autostatic thread, ensuring a secure closure at the mesenteric margin to prevent anastomotic leakage. The mesocolon and mesoideal breach are closed to prevent internal hernias.
The surgical procedure concludes with the removal of the resected specimen through an incision made according to the Fannesty technique.
